Introduction
Osteoporosis consists of a heterogeneous group of syndromes in 
which bone mass per unit volume is reduced in otherwise 
healthy bone, resulting in fragile bone. The rise in bone porosity, 
microarchitectural compromise, and reduced bone quality in -
creases the likelihood of fracture that is generally associated with 
trauma, especially with falls. The mineral-matrix ratio is normal 
in osteoporosis, but the mineral content is reduced in osteoma -
lacia, which can be associated with osteoporosis. Osteoporosis is 
the most prevalent metabolic bone disease in the United States 
and is a major public health concern. The direct and indirect 
costs of osteoporosis in the United States alone are estimated to 
be more than $14 billion annually. 21 Much of this expense re -
lates to hip fractures. In 15% to 20% of hip fracture cases, the 
outcome is fatal. The World Health Organization (WHO) has 
defined osteoporosis as bone mineral density (BMD) 2.5 or 
more standard deviations (SD) below the peak mean bone mass 
of healthy adults. 1 The T-score is the amount of one’s bone den-
sity compared with the mean for healthy young adults of the 
same sex. The Z-score is calculated in the same way, but the 
comparison is made with someone of the same age, sex, race, 
height, and weight. The Z-score is adjusted for an individual’s 
age, but the T-score is not. For example, a 75-year-old female 
with a Z-score of 21 is 1 SD below the BMD of an average 
75-year-old female, but her T-score may be 23 because she is  
3 SD below the BMD of an average 35-year-old female. Normal 
BMD is defined as a T-score of 21 or greater; osteopenia, a  
T-score between 21 and 22.5; osteoporosis, a T-score of 22.5 
or less; and severe osteoporosis, a T-score of 22.5 or less with a 
fragility fracture. In the asymptomatic stage, osteoporosis is 
characterized simply by decreased bone density without frac -
ture. Osteoporosis becomes clinically problematic only when 
fractures occur.
• The Bone Health and Osteoporosis Foundation recommends 
that individuals who have risk factors for osteoporosis have a 
bone mineral density evaluation. This group includes premature 
menopause with risk factors, females age 65 years or older and 
males age 70 years or older regardless of risk factors, females in 
the postmenopausal stage of life who have at least one risk 
factor for osteoporosis, individuals who have sustained a 
fracture (especially fragility fractures), and people who have a 
vertebral abnormality indicative of fracture or take medications 
associated with bone loss and fractures, such as prednisone.
• Conditions associated with osteoporosis and fractures include 
primary hyperparathyroidism, type 1 and type 2 diabetes 
mellitus, various forms of liver disease, chronic kidney disease, 
hyperthyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, cortisol excess, celiac 
disease, and multiple myeloma.
• Risk factors for bone loss and fractures include cigarette smok -
ing, excess alcohol consumption, low body weight, and paren-
teral history of hip fracture.
35
Osteoporosis
MEHRSHEED SINAKI, ROBERT A. WERMERS, AND CARA C. PRIDEAUX
• Approximately 10% of bone tissue is replaced annually to main -
tain its biomechanical competence.
• Peak adult bone mass is achieved between the ages of 30 and 
35 years; age-related bone loss is a universal phenomenon in 
humans.
• Optimal nutrition and physical activity are necessary to 
achieve the genetic potential for bone mass. Nutrition can 
also affect both bone matrix formation and bone 
mineralization.
• There is strong evidence that moderate-intensity resistance 
training and impact exercise can decrease the risk of vertebral 
fracture in osteoporosis and osteopenia. Balance training, 
weight-bearing aerobic exercise, and physical activity can also 
be effective, but exercise recommendations should always be 
individualized.
• The greatest osteogenic effect from mechanical loading occurs 
when the strain is vigorous, repeated daily, short in duration, 
and applied to a specific bone site.

SECTION 4  Issues in Specific Diagnoses
Bone Function and Structure
Bone serves as a mechanical support for musculoskeletal struc -
tures, as protection for vital organs, and as a metabolic source of 
ions, especially calcium and phosphate. Despite its appearance, 
bone is a metabolically active tissue. T o maintain its biomechani-
cal competence, bone undergoes continuous renewal in adults so 
that older bone tissue is replaced by newly formed bone tissue. 
Approximately 10% of bone tissue is replaced annually by this 
cyclic process in adults. There are two types of bone cells: osteo -
clasts, which resorb the calcified matrix, and osteoblasts, which 
synthesize the new bone matrix. 78 Osteoclasts are localized on 
bone surfaces. Their origin is hematopoietic, and they share a 
common precursor with the monocyte macrophage. Osteoclasts 
are large, multinucleated cells. They have a special cell membrane 
with folds that invaginate at the interface with bone surface, 
called the ruffled border. T o induce resorption of bone and the 
mineralized bone matrix, osteoclasts produce proteolytic enzymes 
in this ruffled border.
Osteoblasts are derived from mesenchymal stem cells. The role 
of osteoblasts is to mineralize the matrix through vesicle budding 
from the cytoplasmic membrane. These vesicles are rich in alka -
line phosphatase. Osteoblasts secrete all growth factors that are 
trapped in the matrix.
Bone Remodeling
Bone remodeling is a process that removes old bone tissue and 
replaces it with new bone tissue. This process maintains the bio -
mechanical integrity of the skeleton and supports the role of bone 
in the provision of an ionic bank for body and mechanical sup -
port. Bone remodeling has five phases:
 1. Activation: Osteoclastic activity is recruited.
 2. Resorption: Osteoclasts erode bone and form a cavity.
 3. Reversal: Osteoblasts are recruited.
 4. Formation: Osteoblasts replace the cavity with new bone.
 5. Quiescence: Bone tissue remains dormant until the next cycle 
starts.
The bone remodeling process is cyclic, starting with bone 
resorption and finishing with bone formation. In adult human 
bone, each cycle of remodeling lasts 3 to 12 months. After bone 
resorption, the reversal phase begins; this involves osteoblastic 
activity. Osteoblasts start to fill the resorption cavity. During the 
process of osteoclastic activity, the growth factors that are stored 
in the bone matrix are released and subsequently stimulate 
osteoblastic proliferation.
The process of bone resorption and formation is called cou -
pling. The ideal situation in the coupling process is equilibrated 
bone formation and resorption. In osteoporosis, however, there is 
disequilibrium between resorption and formation, favoring re -
sorption and resulting in net bone loss. The number of active re -
modeling units in trabecular bone is approximately three times 
greater than in cortical bone. The physical endurance of any bone 
is affected by the percentage of cortical bone involved in its struc-
ture. T rabecular bone is more active metabolically than cortical 
bone because of the greater amount of remodeling surfaces. Con-
sequently, more bone loss may occur at the trabecular areas when 
resorption is greater than formation in certain conditions. The 
vertebrae consist of 50% trabecular bone and 50% cortical bone, 
whereas the femoral neck consists of 30% trabecular bone and  
70% cortical bone.
Pathogenesis
Peak adult bone mass is achieved between the ages of 30 and  
35 years. Bone mass at any point thereafter is the difference be -
tween peak adult bone mass and the amount that has been lost 
since the peak was reached. Age-related bone loss is a universal 
phenomenon in humans. Any circumstances that reduce bone 
formation or increase bone resorption increase the likelihood that 
osteoporosis will develop later in life. Measures that can maximize 
peak adult bone mass are clearly desirable. T rabecular (or cancel-
lous) bone represents approximately 20% of skeletal bone mass 
and makes up 80% of the turnover media. The cortex makes up 
only 20% of the turnover media and is made of compact bone, 
which represents 80% of skeletal bone mass. In both cortical and 
trabecular bone, bone remodeling is initiated with the activation 
of osteoclasts. The resulting resorption sites are then refilled by 
osteoblastic activities, a process called bone formation. If the 
amount of bone resorbed equals the amount formed, the bone 
loss is zero. The remodeling process does not result in a zero bal -
ance after the age of 30 to 35 years; after this age, the normal 
process of remodeling results in net bone loss. 74
Certain conditions, such as hyperparathyroidism and thyrotoxi-
cosis, can increase the rate of bone remodeling. These conditions 
increase the rate of bone loss and the risk of osteoporosis develop-
ment. Many secondary causes of osteoporosis are associated with an 
increased rate of activation of the remodeling cycle. Other condi-
tions, such as glucocorticoid excess, predominantly reduce bone 
formation, which also increases the risk of osteoporosis. Although 
factors such as calcium intake, smoking, alcohol consumption, 
physical exercise, and menopause are important in determining 
BMD, genetic factors are the major determinant and contribute to 
80% of the variance in peak BMD.30 Fracture incidence related to 
osteoporosis is lower in males than in females because the diameter 
of vertebral bodies and long bones is greater in males at maturity, 
and bone loss is less (about half that of females) throughout life.80
Classification of Osteoporosis
Osteoporosis can be primary or secondary to other disorders  
that result in bone loss. Typical causes of osteoporosis are listed 
in Box 35.1 . The most common type of osteoporosis is either 
postmenopausal or age related. 65 Primary osteoporosis is a rare 
disorder, also known as idiopathic juvenile osteoporosis. This 
type of osteoporosis typically occurs before puberty (at age 
8–14 years), and patients present with osteoporosis that is pro -
gressive over 2 to 4 years in association with multiple axial or 
axioappendicular fractures. Remission usually occurs by the 
end of the 2- to 4-year course. 50 In this type of osteoporosis, the 
process of bone formation is normal, but osteoclastic activity 
increases, resulting in increased bone resorption. Idiopathic 
juvenile osteoporosis is most evident in the thoracic and lumbar 
spine and needs to be distinguished from juvenile epiphysitis or 
Scheuermann disease. Idiopathic juvenile osteoporosis is usu -
ally self-limiting, but the radiographic appearance may not re -
turn to normal. The laboratory values are typically normal, and 
diagnosis is made by exclusion.
Hormones and Physiology of Bone
The rate of bone remodeling can be increased by parathyroid hor-
mone (PTH), thyroxine, growth hormone, and active vitamin D

(calcitriol; 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D 3 [1,25(OH) 2D3]) and de -
creased by calcitonin, estrogen, and glucocorticoids.57
The primary hormone for calcium homeostasis is PTH. The 
level of plasma calcium is the major moderator for the secretion 
of PTH, which regulates the plasma calcium ion (Ca 21) concen-
tration in three ways:
 1. In the presence of active vitamin D, PTH stimulates bone 
resorption and the release of calcium and phosphate.
 2. Through production of calcitriol in the kidneys, PTH indi -
rectly increases intestinal absorption of calcium and phosphate.
 3. PTH increases active reabsorption of Ca 21 in the renal distal 
tubal area.
PTH also reduces proximal tubular reabsorption of phosphate. 
In general, PTH increases serum calcium and decreases serum 
phosphate concentration. Calcitonin is a hormone secreted by the 
parafollicular cells of the thyroid gland. The major stimulus of 
calcitonin production is the serum level of calcium. Calcitonin 
directly prohibits calcium and phosphate resorption through in -
hibition of osteoclastic activity, lowering the serum calcium level. 
The main regulators of vitamin D synthesis are the serum concen-
trations of 1,25(OH) 2D3, calcium, phosphate, PTH, and fibro -
blast growth factor-23. Vitamin D can also be obtained through 
diet and supplements or synthesized through cutaneous sun expo-
sure with subsequent conversion in the liver to 25-hydroxyvita -
min D. PTH is the major inducer of production of the active 
form of vitamin D in the kidney. This function is accomplished 
through the effect of the enzyme 1-alpha-hydroxylase, which 
transforms 25-hydroxyvitamin D to the active form, calcitriol. 
Calcitriol increases intestinal absorption of calcium and phos -
phate. Vitamin D is essential for appropriate bone mineralization.
Role of Sex Steroids
The main endocrine function that occurs at menopause is loss of 
the secretion of estrogen and progesterone from the ovaries. 57,80 
Both estrogen and progesterone stimulate osteoblastic activity. The 
premenopausal ovary primarily produces estradiol. Progesterone 
secretion, which occurs cyclically after ovulation in the premeno-
pausal stage, also decreases to very low levels in the postmeno -
pausal stage. These changes in circulating sex steroids are gradual 
throughout a females’ sexual reproductive life. The premenopausal 
ovary also produces androgens, especially testosterone. The circu-
lating testosterone levels decrease after menopause. The major 
source of estrogen in postmenopausal females is conversion from 
dehydroepiandrosterone. The latter is then converted into andro-
stenedione, which changes into estrone in fat cells. Estrone is the 
major source of estrogen in postmenopausal females.
Males do not have the equivalent of menopause, but in some 
elderly males, bone mass decreases along with a decline in gonadal 
function. The testosterone level in males decreases with age as a 
result of a decreased number of Leydig cells in the testes. Male 
hypogonadism is typically associated with bone loss. 66
Other Factors Affecting Bone Mass
Several other factors can contribute to the reduction of sex-related 
steroid levels. In hyperprolactinemia, which can be attributable to 
a prolactin-secreting pituitary tumor, for example, failure of the 
gonadal axis results in a substantial loss of bone. Amenorrheic 
athletes who exercise excessively, such as high-mileage runners or 
ballet dancers, who have lower-than-normal body weight, have 
lower circulating estradiol, progesterone, and prolactin levels. 
Their amenorrhea is associated with hypothalamic hypogonad -
ism, which leads to excessive bone loss. This bone loss can be 
mostly reversed when training distances or times are decreased. 36 
With weight gain and improvement in nutrition, these athletes 
can facilitate resumption of menses and reversal of bone loss.17,36,120 
Reduction of sex steroid concentrations is not the only cause of 
bone loss. Other factors such as race, genetics, nutrition, amount 
of physical exercise, and lifestyle can also contribute to the rate of 
bone loss after an ovariectomy or natural menopause. 81 It is well 
known that bone must be physically stressed to be maintained. A 
considerable amount of data show that the rate of change in strain 
also influences bone growth and remodeling.62
Effect of Aging on Bone Mass
In the normal aging process, there is an imbalance between re -
sorption and formation of bone because osteoblastic activity is not 
equal to osteoclastic activity. The result of the remodeling process 
is bone loss during each individual cycle of remodeling. Bone loss 
occurs even when the remodeling process is not increased. In fact,

ing process. This decreased activation gives rise to the concept of 
low-turnover osteoporosis, which occurs concomitantly with the 
aging process. Age plays a considerable role in the rate of bone 
turnover. It has been determined that bone turnover increases in 
females during menopause, but bone turnover does not increase 
substantially in males with aging. Most studies have shown that 
plasma levels of the active form of vitamin D, 1,25(OH)
2D3, 
decrease with age by approximately 50% in both sexes.
Growth hormone stimulates renal production of 1,25(OH)2D3. 
The production of growth hormone decreases with age. Secretion 
of growth hormone is reduced in patients with osteoporosis. 
Growth hormone and insulin-like growth factor-1 have several 
positive effects on calcium homeostasis, including synthesis of 
1,25(OH)
2D3, osteoblast proliferation, osteoclast formation, and 
bone resorption. Calcium absorption has also been shown to be 
less efficient in those who are elderly.
47 Plasma calcitonin levels are 
higher in males than in females, and they do not change with age. 
Studies have shown that estrogens stimulate calcitonin secre -
tion.
115 Thyroid hormone levels typically show no change or only 
slightly decrease with age. PTH levels increase with age, perhaps 
because of reduced renal function and decreased 1,25(OH)
2D3 
concentration.15 This reduction in the active form of vitamin D 
can be a result of decreased consumption of dietary vitamin D, 
decreased exposure to sunlight, decreased skin capacity for vita -
min D conversion, reduced intestinal absorption, and reduced 
1-alpha-hydroxylase activity. Studies have shown that the overall 
level of physical activity decreases with aging.
82,91 This is impor-
tant to note because physical strain and mechanical load positively 
affect bone mass.
38 For example, female gymnasts, both children 
and college-aged athletes, reportedly have higher BMD than 
swimmers.
16,34 Exercise is known to stimulate the release of 
growth hormone or other trophic factors that can stimulate osteo-
blastic activity.35 Optimal nutrition and physical activity are nec -
essary to achieve the genetic potential for bone mass. Peak bone 
mass attained by early adulthood is a major determinant of bone 
mass in later life. Nutrition can also affect both bone matrix for -
mation and bone mineralization. Vitamin K is a cofactor for 
metabolism of osteocalcin, an important component of the bone 
extracellular matrix. 98 In general, in females who are estrogen 
deficient, daily calcium intake of 1200 mg/day is recommended. 
The recommended dietary allowance for vitamin D is 600 inter -
national units (IU; 15 mg) in the general population, increasing 
to 800 IU (20 mg) for those age 70 years or older. Empiric vita -
min D supplementation is suggested for those age 75 years and 
older given the potential mortality benefit.
26
Clinical Manifestations of Osteoporosis
Osteoporosis is known as a “silent disease” until fractures occur. 
Osteoporotic vertebral fractures can go unnoticed until they are 
incidentally seen on unrelated imaging such as chest x-ray or spine 
imaging. Appendicular fractures, however, typically require im -
mediate attention. The fact that a fracture results from osteoporo-
sis should not affect the orthopedic method of management. The 
most common areas for osteoporotic fractures are the midthoracic 
and upper lumbar spine (Fig. 35.1),
108 hip (proximal femur), and 
distal forearm (Colles fracture). The highest incidence of fractures 
is in White females. The female-male ratio is approximately 7:1 
for vertebral fractures, 2:1 for hip fractures, and 5:1 for Colles 
fractures. It has been estimated that after menopause, a female’s 
lifetime risk of sustaining an osteoporotic fracture is 1 in 2 or 3.
60
Hip fracture is the greatest concern clinically because the risk 
of death with osteoporotic hip fracture is 15% to 20%. This is 
despite all the modern developments in surgical and nonsurgical 
intervention. The management of an osteoporotic spine fracture 
requires immobilization of the involved vertebral bodies and an -
algesia. Fortunately, these fractures heal through, becoming more 
condensed; unlike appendicular fractures, they typically do not 
require any specific treatment. If there is nonunion of the appen-
dicular fracture, then one should consider secondary causes of 
osteoporosis. The duration of immobilization should be for only 
a limited time, sufficient to ensure the primary fracture-healing 
process. Prolonged immobilization is discouraged because it can 
contribute to additional osteoporosis. The orthopedic manage -
ment of most osteoporotic fractures is generally noncontroversial, 
except for the management of hip fracture. The management of 
femoral neck fracture creates a great deal of controversy because

controversies through prospective studies. Despite these efforts, 
the treatment of hip fracture remains a challenge, and each case 
creates an emergency situation. Shoulder fracture, especially frac -
ture in the surgical neck of the proximal humerus, is not uncom-
mon in elderly females. This type of fracture usually occurs from 
impact force directly to the shoulder during a fall. A conservative 
treatment regimen typically suffices for this fracture.
Fractures and Management
The relationship between bone mass and spinal fractures has been 
extensively studied, and it is known that fracture risk increases as 
bone mass decreases. For every standard deviation of decrease in 
BMD, the risk of osteoporotic fracture of the spine increases  
1.5- to 2-fold, and the risk of hip fracture increases 2.6-fold.
45 
Another predictor of fracture risk is age itself. The risk of fracture 
as a result of osteoporosis doubles every 5 to 7 years.
45 It is not 
clear whether age-related changes in bone density or bone quality 
are factors that increase the risk of fractures caused by falls.
Vertebral Fractures
The incidence of vertebral fractures is poorly understood because 
50% of these fractures can be subclinical, and the patient may not 
seek medical attention. Vertebral fractures can create both acute 
and chronic pain. Acute pain that occurs in the absence of a previ-
ous fracture is usually a result of compression fractures of the 
vertebrae. Sometimes, a minor fall or even a strong hug can cause 
a compression fracture. The compressed vertebrae might not be 
apparent on radiographs for up to 4 weeks after the injury.
65 
Compression fractures usually result in acute pain that later re -
solves (Box 35.2). 90 The spinal deformity that may result from 
these fractures can produce chronic pain.90
Kyphotic postural change is the most physically disfiguring and 
psychologically damaging effect of osteoporosis.103 The incidence 
of osteoporosis and kyphosis can be substantially decreased by 
early detection and subsequent intervention in high-risk patients.
Disproportionate weakness in back extensor musculature rela-
tive to body weight or spinal flexor strength considerably increases 
the possibility of compressing the vertebrae in a fragile osteopo -
rotic spine. Recognition and improvement of decreased back ex -
tensor strength can enhance the ability to maintain proper vertical 
alignment.
89 The geriatric population has an increased risk of de-
bilitating postural changes caused by several factors, the two most 
apparent being a greater prevalence of osteoporosis and an invo -
lutional loss of functional muscle motor units.
28,59 Development 
of kyphotic posture not only can predispose to postural back pain 
but also can increase the risk of falls.
58 Several additional factors 
can also contribute to the risk for falls (Box 35.3).
Chronic spinal pain can be attributable to a deformity caused 
by vertebral wedging and compression and by secondary ligamen-
tous strain. These deformities are often difficult to distinguish 
from the typically associated disc deterioration. The intervertebral 
discs undergo the most dramatic age-related changes of all con-
nective tissues.
2 With aging, there is an increase in the number 
and diameter of the collagen fibrils in the disc. This change is 
accompanied by a progressive decrease in disc resilience, and a loss 
of distinction between the nucleus pulposus and the annulus fi-
brosus eventually occurs. Chronic back pain secondary to osteo-
porosis is related to postural changes resulting from vertebral 
fractures.
103,104 Strong back muscles contribute to good posture 
and skeletal support (Fig. 35.2). 48,113,114 One controlled study

Factors Contributing to Risk of Falls
Extrinsic
• Environmental: obstac les, slippery floors, uneven surfaces, poor illumina-
tion, stairs not well defined, pets, icy sidewalks
• Extraskeletal: ina ppropriate footwear, obstructive clothing
Intrinsic
• Intraskeletal: lo wer extremity weakness (neurogenic or myopathic), balance 
disorder (vestibular disequilibrium, peripheral neuropathy, hyperkyphosis), 
visual impairment, bifocal use, vestibular changes, cognitive decline, de-
creased coordination (cerebellar degeneration), postural changes, imbal-
ance, gait unsteadiness, gait apraxia, reduced muscle strength, reduced 
flexibility, orthopnea, postural hypotension, cardiovascular deconditioning, 
iatrogenically reduced alertness

Management of Acute Pain in Patients 
With Osteoporosis
• Bedrest (2 days): substantial bone loss is not likely to occur with 2 days of 
bedrest
• Analgesics: a void constipating medicines, such as codeine derivatives
• Avoidance of constipation
• Physical therapy:  initially cold packs, then mild heat and stroking massage
• Avoidance of exertional exercises
• Knowledge of lifting and standing principles to avoid excessive spinal strain
• Back supports if needed to decrease pain and expedite ambulation
• Gait aids if needed

8 years after cessation of the exercise. 105 No female in either the 
control or the exercise group in the study received hormone replace-
ment therapy. Compared with the exercise group, the control group 
had a 2.7 times greater number of vertebral fractures at 10-year

105 The pain and skeletal deformity associated 
with osteoporosis may secondarily reduce muscle strength. The  
reduction in muscle strength can further exacerbate postural abnor-
malities associated with this condition (Fig. 35.3).48 Chronic pain 
can also be attributable to microfractures that are visible only on 
bone scan and can occur continuously. Management of chronic 
osteoporosis-related pain is outlined in Box 35.4. Prescription of 
opiate analgesics should be undertaken judiciously because their use 
can cause constipation.
90 If absolutely necessary, they should be 
used for the shortest duration possible, likely only 1 or 2 days. After 
a 10-year follow-up study,
105 Sinaki et al. developed the following 
hypothesis: “Back resistive exercises performed in a prone position 
(nonvertical loading) rather than in vertical loading position can 
decrease risk of vertebral fractures through improvement of hori -
zontal trabeculae.”
86 Exercise needs to be progressive, resistive, and 
nonvertical loading to avoid vertebral compression fracture.
Vertebroplasty and Kyphoplasty
Vertebroplasty and kyphoplasty procedures are used for the man-
agement of vertebral fractures.29,51,94 These procedures involve the 
injection of acrylic cement (such as polymethylmethacrylate) into 
a partially collapsed vertebral body. Jensen et al.
49 found that 63% 
of patients with osteoporosis who underwent vertebroplasty  
decreased their use of opiates and analgesics for pain control,  
7% increased their use, and 30% continued on the same amount. 
More recently, two multicenter, randomized controlled trials 
(RCTs) evaluating vertebroplasty demonstrated no substantial dif-
ference in pain relief when compared with a sham procedure.
14,51 
Vertebroplasty does not substitute for rehabilitative measures that 
are needed after a fracture.
46,70 One study showed considerably 
fewer vertebral refractures after vertebroplasty in patients who 
received instruction for back extension exercises (Fig. 35.4).
46 The 
author recommends a rehabilitation program, especially back  
extension exercises, for osteoporosis management.94
Hip Fracture
Falls and hip fractures can be life threatening.64,116 In addition to 
weakness of the lower limbs, one of the contributing factors to 
falls in individuals with spinal kyphotic posture is disequilib -
rium.
99 The kyphotic posture places the center of gravity closer to 
the limit of stability. 100 Measures that reduce instability, such as 
exercise programs for equilibrium, including tai chi, some yoga 
poses,
88 and use of a weighted kypho-orthosis (WKO), as in the 
Spinal Proprioceptive Extension Exercise Dynamic (SPEED) pro-
gram, can reduce both the fear of falls and the overall risk of falls 
(Fig. 35.5).
100 Hip fracture is an emergency situation. In typical 
cases, the limb is rotated outward (externally rotated) and short -
ened. It is difficult to tell from clinical evaluation whether the 
fracture is intracapsular (femoral neck fracture) or extracapsular 
(trochanteric fracture). Radiographs are necessary to make this

throplasties. The hemiarthroplasty, however, is considerably less 
surgical trauma for the patient and is advocated for very old or 
frail patients with a prognosis of limited mobility.
The trochanteric hip fracture creates fewer problems, despite 
the fact that the fracture engages more bone than a femoral neck 
fracture. The operative treatment of choice is internal fixation 
(Fig. 35.6). The postoperative course for all hip fractures, regard-
less of whether internal fixation or joint arthroplasty is done, is 
less eventful if physical therapeutic measures are used postopera-
tively, including the use of gait aids with partial weight bearing on 
the operative side. Only for patients with severely comminuted 
fractures, or fractures in which the operative result has been un -
satisfactory, is the restriction to no weight bearing needed.
27
Hip Pads for Fracture Prophylaxis
There is conflicting evidence as to whether hip protectors can re -
duce the incidence of hip fractures in the elderly, high-risk popu-
lation. Compliance with use of hip protectors has been a concern 
in the nursing home population. One study showed no substan-
tial difference in the incidence of hip fractures, even in the par -
ticipants who were compliant with the use of hip protectors.
117 
However, it appears that at-risk elderly individuals, especially 
those with a history of falls, impaired balance, and decreased cog-
nition, would benefit from the use of hip pads in addition to the 
use of gait aids.
85 In addition, rehabilitation for patients to learn 
how to fall and land safely can decrease the risk of hip fracture 
resulting from high-impact contact during a fall. Landing on the 
buttocks is less traumatic to hips than landing on the greater tro -
chanters.
77
Sacral Insufficiency Fracture
Other axial skeletal fractures, such as fractures of the sacral alae 
and pubic rami, can also occur (Fig. 35.7). Pelvic fractures are 
particularly common in patients with osteoporosis. Fractures of 
the pubic rami can occur with minimal strain, and most patients 
can hardly recall having a traumatic event or an incident of severe 
strain that would have caused such a fracture. Healing typically 
occurs without invasive procedures. Ambulatory activities are re -
duced temporarily, and a wheeled walker is initially recommended 
to decrease pain. Later in the course of treatment, crutches and a 
cane can be used. Weight bearing must be limited, as dictated by 
the level of pain in the pelvic area. Fracture of the sacrum with 
minimal trauma can also occur, and the goal of management is to 
decrease weight-bearing pain with use of proper assistive devices 
for ambulation.
90 For management of pelvic pain, physical thera-
peutic measures are recommended.
Diagnostic Studies in Osteoporosis
The Bone Health and Osteoporosis Foundation recommends that 
individuals who have risk factors for osteoporosis have a BMD 
evaluation.

females age 65 years or older and males age 70 years or older re -
gardless of risk factors, females in the postmenopausal stage of life 
who have at least one risk factor for osteoporosis, individuals who 
have sustained a fracture (especially fragility fractures), and people 
who have a vertebral abnormality indicative of fracture or

fractures include primary hyperparathyroidism, type 1 and type 2 
diabetes mellitus, various forms of liver disease, chronic kidney 
disease, hyperthyroidism, rheumatoid arthritis, cortisol excess, 
celiac disease, and multiple myeloma. Other risk factors for bone 
loss and fractures include cigarette smoking, excess alcohol con -
sumption, low body weight, and parenteral history of hip frac-
ture. A follow-up BMD study can be considered after 2 years or 
longer, depending on the baseline BMD and estimated fracture 
risk.
56 Bone turnover markers may also be measured for addi -
tional information on medication efficacy and compliance if an 
earlier assessment of medication efficacy is desired (i.e., 3 months).
The diagnosis of osteoporosis requires a thorough history 
and physical examination, including family history of osteopo -
rosis, type and location of musculoskeletal pain, general  
dietary calcium intake, height and weight measurements, func -
tional assessment, dental health, fall history, and level of 
physical activity ( Table 35.1). Several biochemical indices are 
also used in the differential diagnosis of metabolic bone disease 
or, in some instances, for therapeutic follow-up.
25 Biochemical 
markers for skeletal health include calcium, phosphorus, PTH, 
25-hydroxyvitamin D, alkaline phosphatase, and 24-hour uri -
nary calcium. Bone turnover markers may be helpful in the 
assessment of metabolic bone disorders and to assess high bone 
turnover stages (see Table 35.1).
Radiographic findings of osteoporosis on vertebral x-rays in -
clude increased lucency of the vertebral bodies with loss of hori -
zontal trabeculae, increased prominence of the cortical end plates, 
vertically oriented trabeculae, reduced cortex thickness, and loss 
of vertebral height, including anterior wedging of vertebral bodies 
(Fig. 35.8).
39 The degree of wedging that indicates a true fracture 
varies from a 15% to 25% reduction in anterior height relative to 
the posterior height of the same vertebra. Other morphological 
changes occur, including biconcavity of vertebral bodies and  
complete compression fractures (reduction in both anterior and 
posterior heights by $25% compared with adjacent normal ver -
tebrae).
39 Spine computed tomography (CT) scans and magnetic 
resonance imaging (MRI) can further define vertebral abnormali-
ties and assess acuity of such changes (Fig. 35.9) if needed. Osteo-
porosis is typically not visible on conventional radiographs until 
at least 25% to 30% of bone mineral has been lost. Consequently, 
evaluation of BMD through absorptiometry techniques is recom-
mended.
119 These measurements are also helpful in assessing 
changes in bone density with therapeutic intervention. The differ-
ent methods for evaluation of bone mass have differing levels of 
precision. Other available methods include photon absorptiome -
try (single or dual), finger x-ray spectrometry, ultrasound densi -
tometry, qualitative CT , high-resolution peripheral quantitative 
CT scans, and dual-energy x-ray absorptiometry (DEXA). The 
most commonly used technique is DEXA; it has high precision 
and is frequently used for research and clinical evaluations to 
measure BMD of the spine, hips, and wrist ( Fig. 35.10). The 
amount of radiation used with this technique is less than 3 mrad. 
The BMD of the femoral neck is often a preferred measure
119 
because spine bone density can be erroneously high as a result of 
osteoarthritis of the spine.

Treatment
Osteoporosis treatment is best done with a team approach be -
cause it is a multifactorial condition.10 For the cause to be defined 
and proper pharmacotherapy and physical interventions to be 
initiated, a provider with expertise in osteoporosis management 
may be beneficial, along with interventions from specialists in 
physical medicine and rehabilitation, pharmacology, psychology, 
and nutrition. Interested physical therapists and occupational 
therapists can be of great help with these interventions. The 
WHO has defined osteoporosis as a T-score of 22.5 or less. De-
tailed definitions of normal BMD, osteopenia, and osteoporosis 
are provided earlier in the text of this chapter. These definitions 
facilitate decision making for therapeutic trials.
Although there are studies on the whole-body vibration effect 
on BMD, the quality of evidence is low, and there is a need for 
further long-term RCTs with adequate sample size.
24 Additional 
studies are also needed on the long-term effect of whole-body vi -
bration on the central nervous system
Fractures may occur with falls; therefore the prevention of falls 
decreases the risk of fracture. Falls are multifactorial. 85 Intactness 
of several central and peripheral nervous system factors is required 
for steadiness of gait.
98 A controlled trial showed that patients 
with osteoporosis who had kyphosis were at substantially higher 
at risk for falls than controls without osteoporosis/kyphosis.
99 
Considerable improvement in the risk for falls is demonstrated by 
the SPEED program. This program produced a decreased step 
width, improved steadiness of gait-on-gait laboratory testing, de-
creased the risk for falls with obstacles, and increased velocity, 
cadence, and stride length in individuals.
100 Through the use of a 
WKO and SPEED program, back pain decreased, and the level of

Exercise
The efficacy of exercise for improving bone mass is supported by 
hormonal and nutritional factors (Box 35.5). T o meet the chal-
lenge of mechanical load, skeletal tissue must have enough bone 
mass and proper architecture to withstand physical strain that is 
imposed on it. Normal musculoskeletal structure is highly adapt -
able and can meet the challenge of usual mechanical loads. Me -
chanical loading considerably influences bone remodeling. Disuse 
or lack of loading may result in acceleration of bone turnover with 
bone loss caused by resorption beyond formation. This type of 
loss can occur in patients confined to bed after an injury to the 
brain or spine.
123 This type of bone loss is observed in astronauts 
who spend extended periods of time in low-gravity environ -
ments.67 The challenges of mechanical load and strain may not be 
tolerated in those with osteopenia and osteoporosis without caus-
ing damage to the architecture of bone. Therefore, in our study, 
to perform loading of the spine without causing vertebral fracture, 
the spine was exposed to a gradual, progressive mechanical load in 
the supine position, where the horizontal trabeculae were loaded 
(Fig. 35.11).
86
A supervised, nonstrenuous, progressive, resistive exercise 
program can improve bone mass in inactive individuals. 112 By 
understanding both the benefits and the challenges of nutritional 
and exercise approaches for musculoskeletal management of  
osteoporosis, we can create an improved prophylactic program 
for patients with osteoporosis.

has to be individualized. Fitness programs, such as swimming or 
short periods of stationary biking, are not sufficiently osteo-
genic
68 but can fulfill the need for cardiovascular fitness without

87,112
Posture Training Program and the Osteoporotic 
Skeletal Frame
Any support that can improve posture and decrease pain-related 
paraspinal muscle cocontraction is desirable. The number and 
diameter of collagen fibrils in the intervertebral discs decrease 
with aging. This reduction results in loss of resiliency of the discs. 
The effect of aging on intervertebral discs is substantial. The disc 
collagens and proteoglycans undergo quantitative and qualitative 
changes.
2 This disc degeneration related to aging is clinically 
greater at L4/L5 than L2/L3 and could be clinically asymptom -
atic.11 In addition, reduced paraspinal muscle strength96,109 and 
forward tendency of the head and trunk related to the effect of 
gravity can cause neck and upper back pain as well as iliocostal 
friction syndrome and flank pain.
87 Cervicalgia and related head-
aches are not uncommon in severe kyphosis (Fig. 35.20). 13,98
Iliocostal pain (see Fig. 35.20) does not respond to the use  
of conventional orthoses. Indeed, orthoses such as corsets can 
make the pain worse through pressure over the lower ribcage  
(Fig. 35.21). Posture training programs that are intended to de-
crease kyphosis can also subsequently reduce iliocostal friction 
syndrome.
52,82,97 One study showed that use of a WKO and back 
exercise increased back strength more significantly (P ,.02) than 
back exercise alone. 52 The same study also showed that use of a 
thoracolumbar support interfered with improving back strength 
with exercise (Fig. 35.22). Posture training programs, such as the 
application of a WKO for 20 minutes two to three times a day in 
cases of severe kyphosis or less frequently in milder cases, while 
contracting the back extensors, can provide reeducation of the 
paraspinal muscles for improvement of kyphotic posturing and 
fall risk reduction (Fig. 35.23).
107 In some severe cases, back ex-
tension is not possible without use of a WKO. Furthermore, 
proper use of a WKO can decrease back and flank pain in patients 
with osteoporosis (Fig. 35.24).
52,69,82,84,97
Orthoses and the Osteoporotic Spine
Acute compression fractures usually result in severe pain and, if not 
managed well, can lead to prolonged immobility. The final outcome 
of a compression fracture can be the creation of chronic pain behav-
ior and subsequent psychological consequences. Acute pain needs 
to be actively managed with proper physical measures. Sedative 
stroking massage and initial application of cold, and later heat, 
along with isometric muscle contractions of the paraspinal muscles 
can be helpful. Rigid thoracolumbar orthoses to promote extension 
of the spine are also helpful (Fig. 35.25; also see Chapter 14).
69,84,107 
If thoracolumbar orthoses are not tolerated because of postural 
changes, a thoracic WKO (Fig. 35.26) or a combination of a ky -
pho-orthosis and lower back support (elastic abdominal support) 
may suffice. In some cases, long-distance ambulatory activities may 
require use of a cane or a wheeled walker. T emporary use of a wheel-
chair with a supportive back cushion is sometimes indicated. Every 
effort needs to be taken to prevent additional falls and immobility, 
including having the patient confined to one room or under pro-
longed bedrest. Immobility time should be limited to avoid result-
ing reactive depression and bone loss. Safety during ambulation is 
paramount, and prevention of falls and fracture should be taught in 
the rehabilitative program for patients with osteoporosis. Imple -
mentation of the SPEED program can decrease gait unsteadiness 
and posture-related back pain and increase the overall level of 
physical activity.
100

Pharmacotherapy is essential for improving bone mass, but its ef-
ficacy in the prevention of skeletal deformities depends on muscu-
loskeletal rehabilitation. Over the years, through many studies, the 
efficacy of measures implemented in rehabilitation of osteoporosis

has been substantiated. Of course, the best osteoporosis treatment 
is prevention of falls and vertebral fracture through improving 
muscle strength,
83,84,105 level of physical activity, and peak bone 
mass before the age of 30 years.106 In addition, maintaining some 
level of physical activity throughout life could reduce bone loss.110 
Muscle strength and exercise play substantial roles in the preven -
tion of vertebral fractures105 and falls.83
Adequate calcium intake is required to allow normal bone 
development and to decrease bone loss. Adequate calcium and 
vitamin D intake, however, seems to have only a modest effect on 
bone loss after menopause. Inadequate intake of calcium and 
vitamin D is common, especially in residents of nursing homes. 
One study showed the efficacy of supplementation of calcium and 
vitamin D for reduction of the risk of hip fracture in elderly 
patients.
18 Typical recommendations for females with hormone 
deficiency are 1200 to 1500 mg of elemental calcium between 
diet and supplements if needed and between 800 and 1000 IU of 
vitamin D daily. Combining calcium and vitamin D into a single 
supplement may increase adherence and efficacy.
76
There are two categories of pharmacotherapy for management 
of osteoporosis: (1) antiresorptive agents that slow bone loss and 
(2) anabolic agents that contribute to bone formation. Antire -
sorptive agents include bisphosphonates, calcitonin, denosumab, 
estrogen, and selective estrogen receptor modulators (SERMs). 
Calcium, vitamin D, and bisphosphonates are the most com-
monly advocated pharmacological treatments for involutional 
osteoporosis. Anabolic agents, also known as osteoblast stimulator 
agents, include PTH-based therapy (teriparatide and abalopara -
tide) and romosozumab, which has a dual effect of reducing bone 
resorption and increasing bone formation.

Antiresorptive agents are numerous. Estrogen, for example, 
acts directly on bone cells and has been shown to decrease the 
rate of bone loss and fractures in postmenopausal females, 
whether their menopause is natural or surgical. Estrogen is not as 
commonly utilized based on concerns with risk vs. benefit of 
such therapies and given the availability of more potent osteopo -
rosis therapies.
4,33,40 In a patient with an intact uterus treated 
with estrogen, progesterone should also be used in a proper regi -
men to prevent endometrial hyperplasia and, possibly, endome-
trial carcinoma. There are several regimens for estrogen and 
progesterone use. These agents may be used concurrently (com -
bination pills) or cyclically. The proper regimen needs to be indi-
vidualized.
In estrogen-deficient females if appropriate, minimal effective 
doses of some form of oral estrogen are usually used (conjugated 
equine estrogens, 0.3 mg/day; estradiol, 50 mg/day). Parenteral 
estrogens can be administered as patches, implants, or gels. Im -
plants are not commonly used in the United States. Estrogen 
patches deliver estradiol through the skin and are changed once or 
twice weekly. The advantage of parenteral estrogen is that me -
tabolism in the liver is bypassed, making smaller doses sufficient. 
The strength of the commonly used transdermal estradiol-17 
(patch) can vary from 0.05 mg (usual dose) to 0.1 mg (high dose). 
In some instances, a dose as low as 0.025 mg is effective.
Contraindications to estrogen replacement therapy include 
coronary heart disease, history of thromboembolism or thrombo-
phlebitis, severe active liver disease, undiagnosed vaginal bleeding, 
porphyria cutanea tarda, significant hypertriglyceridemia (with 
oral estrogens), dementia, and suspected breast or endometrial 
carcinoma. Estrogens can also have an adverse effect on existing 
hypertension, hyperlipidemia, migraine headaches, chronic 
thrombophlebitis, and endometriosis. Administration of proges-
tins can result in uncomfortable adverse effects, including fatigue, 
depression, breast tenderness, bloating, menstrual cramps, and 
headaches.
71 Other potential adverse effects are weight gain, 
mood change, increased serum triglyceride and glucose levels, and 
abnormal vaginal bleeding.
Estrogen is most effective for management of vasomotor symp-
toms and genitourinary syndrome of menopause in addition to 
preventing bone loss and fractures. However, many postmeno -
pausal females discontinued their use of hormone replacement 
therapy after the results of the Women’s Health Initiative studies 
became available.
23,71 Of breast cancer cases reported in the 
United States, 78% occur in females after the age of 50 years. 
There are reports that hormone replacement therapy increases the 
risk of breast cancer by 2.3% per year, and this risk increases to 
3.5% per year after 5 years with combination therapy with estro -
gens and progesterone. Females who have cardiovascular disease 
should not use hormone replacement therapy for prevention of 
osteoporosis. Estrogen therapy should also be discontinued if a 
female has an acute cardiovascular event. Importantly, if estrogen 
is initiated in younger individuals within 10 years after meno -
pause, risk of heart disease and all-cause mortality is reduced, but 
the risk of heart disease is potentially higher if initiated in older 
patients more than 10 years after menopause. In the same way, 
there is an increased risk of dementia if combination therapy is 
initiated in patients older than 65 years. The patient’s preference 
is important for management of menopausal symptoms. Preven -
tion of osteoporosis is no longer considered a primary indication 
for the use of estrogen therapy.

tin therapy. Bisphosphonates: Esophagitis with oral bisphosphonates; rare occurrence of 
jaw osteonecrosis and atypical femur fractures with oral and IV bisphosphonates; hypocal -

rare occurrence of jaw osteonecrosis and typical femur fractures. Raloxifene: Deep vein 
thrombosis, hot flashes, nausea, leg cramps, cerebrovascular accident. Calcitonin: See

bIncludes food and supplements.
Raloxifene is a nonsteroidal SERM that has an estrogen ago -
nist effect in bone leading to an increase in BMD and fracture 
reduction at a dose of 60 mg daily.32 Raloxifene has antiestrogenic 
effects in the breast and is approved for reduction of breast cancer 
risk in postmenopausal females.
118 Similar to estrogen, raloxifene 
increases the risk of venous thromboembolism. As opposed to 
estrogen therapy, older females did not demonstrate an increased 
risk of cardiovascular events; although there was a slight increase 
in risk of fatal strokes, there was no overall increase in strokes.
5 
Raloxifene can also worsen hot flashes as opposed to estrogen re -
placement therapy.
Calcitonin, an antiresorptive agent, acts directly on the os -
teoclasts, but it is not used as a first choice for treatment of  
osteoporosis because of adverse effects. Calcitonin has a few 
disadvantages that limit its use,
73 which explains why it is not 
commonly recommended. It is most effective in patients whose 
rate of bone turnover is high. Calcitonin is approved for the 
treatment of established osteoporosis, but the long-term frac -
ture-reducing efficacy has not been clearly demonstrated. Use of 
nasal calcitonin may cause nasal irritation, crusting, and ulcer -
ations, which may require discontinuation of its use.
Alendronate sodium, an amino bisphosphonate, has been 
shown to normalize the rate of bone turnover and increase bone 
mass.
19 Alendronate (10 mg/day or a once-weekly dose of 70 mg) 
must be taken with a full glass of water on awakening. The pa-
tient should not eat or recline for 30 to 45 minutes after taking 
alendronate because of the risk of esophageal irritation. Patient 
education and compliance are important for the proper use. 
Ibandronate sodium is approved for prevention and treatment of 
osteoporosis in postmenopausal females. It can reduce the inci -
dence of vertebral fracture by approximately 50% over 3 years.
21 
Ibandronate sodium is taken orally once monthly as 150-mg 
tablets or as an intravenous injection (over 15–30 seconds) of  
3 mg every 3 months.
21 Bisphosphonates affect trabecular bone, 
especially in the lumbar spine, where BMD increases of 5% to 
10% occur during the first 2 years of treatment ( Table 35.2). 
Alendronate sodium, an amino bisphosphonate, has been shown 
to normalize the rate of bone turnover and increase bone mass.
19 
Risedronate sodium (Actonel) also increases bone density and 
reduces the risk of spine and nonspine fractures by 35% to 45% 
over 3 years. Risedronate is another bisphosphonate that has 
been used for the treatment of osteoporosis. T reatment with rise-
dronate (5 mg/day, 35 mg once weekly, and 150 mg once 
monthly) has been shown to decrease considerably the incidence 
of vertebral and nonvertebral fractures in females with post -
menopausal osteoporosis.
43 Weekly doses of bisphosphonates are 
commonly recommended. One potential adverse effect of oral 
bisphosphonates is esophageal irritation, particularly in patients 
with reflux or other esophageal dysfunction.
21
Zoledronic acid provides significant increases in BMD and re-
duces fracture risk; it is commonly given 5 mg once yearly for  
3 years in lower risk patients up to annually for 6 years in higher 
risk patients.
8 More recent data suggest that zoledronic offers ben-
efit with less frequent dosing intervals. 41,72 Unique risks with 
zoledronic acid include an acute phase reaction that can occur  
in up to 42% of patients, especially with the first infusion. Hypo-
calcemia is also a risk with potent intravenous bisphosphonates. 
Drug holidays with bisphosphonates are recommended after  
5 to 10 years with oral bisphosphonates and 3 to 6 years with 
zoledronic acid because of their prolonged skeletal retention and 
ongoing stability after discontinuation. Drug holidays are not ap-
propriate for any of the nonbisphosphonate therapies. Notably,  
the risk for atypical femur fracture declines rapidly within the first 
year of the drug holiday.
7
Denosumab (Prolia) is approved by the FDA for use in the 
treatment of osteoporosis in postmenopausal females at high risk 
of fracture and to increase bone mass in males with osteoporosis 
at high risk of fracture. It is a RANK ligand inhibitor/human 
monoclonal antibody that reversibly inhibits osteoclast-mediated 
bone resorption. Denosumab should be taken as a single subcuta-
neous injection of 60 mg every 6 months. Denosumab may lower 
serum calcium, especially in patients with preexisting lower cal -

denosumab is not incorporated into the skeletal matrix, and upon 
discontinuation of the medication there is a rapid increase in bone

T eriparatide and abaloparatide are PTH analogues that can 
increase BMD and reduce fracture risk by increasing bone remod-
eling more than bone modeling. 21,61 Both medications are self-
administered through daily subcutaneous injections. T o maintain 
PTH-induced gains after an 18- to 24-month drug therapy 
course, antiresorptive agents need to be considered. 63 PTH ther-
apy is contraindicated in patients with a history of malignancy; 
adverse effects include nausea, dizziness, leg cramps, headache, 
and hypercalcemia. Romosozumab is a monoclonal antibody that 
binds to and inhibits sclerostin thereby inhibiting bone resorption 
and stimulation modeling–based bone formation, leading to in -
creased BMD and reduction in fractures in postmenopausal fe -
males with osteoporosis.20 Romosozumab may increase the risk of 
myocardial infarction, stroke, and cardiovascular death when 
compared with bisphosphonates, and it has been rarely associated 
with osteonecrosis of the jaw and atypical femur fractures. 79
T reatment of osteoporosis in males includes the usual supple-
mentation with calcium (1200–1500 mg/day) and vitamin D 
(800–1000 IU/day), limitation of alcohol use, and cessation of 
smoking. Bisphosphonates have been helpful as antiresorptive 
agents for the management of osteoporosis in males. T eriparatide 
and abaloparatide are also approved for increasing bone mass in 
males.22
A balanced diet is needed for maintenance of musculoskeletal 
health. Excessive dietary intake of sodium and phosphorus should 
be avoided. Certain foods may contribute to bone loss by increas-
ing urinary calcium excretion (e.g., high intake of protein and 
acid-forming substances). In general, 1 to 1.5 g/kg lean body 
weight/day in protein supports normal calcium homeostasis. In -
take of more than 300 mg of caffeine each day (or 18 oz brewed 
coffee in elderly postmenopausal females) has been associated 
with increased risk of hip fracture. 98 Studies of young females 
with malnutrition caused by anorexia nervosa have demonstrated 
irregularity of menstrual periods, estrogen deficiency, poor muscle 
strength, and marked loss of bone mass.
With regard to osteoporosis, the distinct effects of nutrition, 
exercise, hormones, and lifestyle cannot be separated. 84,91 Cessa-
tion of tobacco and alcohol abuse is necessary. Reportedly, there 
was a lower fracture risk in individuals consuming 0.75 to 1.5 oz 
hard liquor per day (beer, 350 mL; 5 oz wine, 140 mL), but in -
take of more than two drinks daily increased the risk by 40%. 6,98 
There are genetic controls of bone, including sex, race, and envi -
ronmental factors (i.e., hormone status, nutrition, age, level of 
physical activity, lifestyle changes).75 A patient’s quality of life can 
certainly be affected by musculoskeletal challenges related to  
osteoporosis.
Summary
Practical treatment of patients with osteoporosis requires pharma-
cological intervention, physical and rehabilitative measures, and 
good nutrition. It also requires consideration of the psychological 
consequences and reactions experienced by patients with osteopo-
rosis. Public education can contribute to prevention, better un -
derstanding, and management of the consequences and challenges 
associated with osteoporosis. For up-to-date information, the 
Bone Health and Osteoporosis Foundation and the International 
Osteoporosis Foundation are excellent educational sources.
